original_abstract:
Microbiological cycling of soil organic matter (SOM) is a fundamental yet poorly understood pillar of the global carbon cycle. Current research suggests that a significant portion of SOM—particularly the dissolved organic matter (DOM) fraction—consists of small molecules derived from lysed cells and microbial metabolites. However, our understanding of the specific composition of these small molecules and the mechanisms governing their movement through the soil environment—whether through biological uptake or mineral adsorption—remains limited. Because the water-extractable organic matter (WEOM) fraction represents the most biologically accessible pool of SOM, it is a critical focus for study.

In this work, we developed a streamlined soil metabolomics workflow and a novel spike-recovery method utilizing $^{13}$C-labeled bacterial lysates to track the persistence of specific metabolites within the WEOM fraction. We evaluated several mass spectrometry-compatible extraction buffers (including water, 10 mM K$_2$SO$_4$, 10 mM NH$_4$HCO$_3$, and various organic solvent mixtures) both with and without prior chloroform vapor fumigation. Using gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) profiling, we identified 55 metabolites by validating their fragmentation patterns and retention times against authentic standards. 

As anticipated, chloroform fumigation significantly increased both the diversity and abundance of recovered metabolites by lysing indigenous microbial cells. To simulate the fate of cell-derived compounds, we spiked soil samples with a $^{13}$C-labeled extract from the soil bacterium *Pseudomonas stutzerii* RCH2. This produced highly enriched metabolites that were easily distinguished from endogenous soil compounds, allowing us to directly compare the composition of a fresh bacterial lysate with the fraction that remains recoverable from the soil matrix.
humanized_abstract:
Microbiological turnover of soil organic matter (SOM) is a vital yet poorly understood component of the global carbon cycle. Recent studies suggest that the dissolved organic matter (DOM) component of SOM is dominated by a cocktail of low molecular weight (LMW) carbon products including a range of bacterial cell-lysate DOM and microbial metabolites. Few data exist on the components of DOM, how these components are degraded via biological or abiotic pathways (for example, microbial uptake, adsorption), and on the detailed controls of WEOM cycling. Consequently, we believe that focusing on the water extractable organic matter (WEOM) fraction of SOM that arguably constitutes the most labile component of SOM would prove to be very rewarding.

Here, we demonstrate a fast and cost effective metabolomics workflow for soil-derived untargeted exometabolome (WEOM) analysis, including a new spike and recovery strategy employing $^{13}$C-labelled bacterial lysates for monitoring the fate of individual soil-derived metabolites in the WEOM. Furthermore, we evaluated the effects of various mass spectrometry compatible extraction media including water, 10 mM K2SO4, 10 mM NH4HCO3, as well as different mixtures of organic solvents alone or in combination, both prior and following fumigation with chloroform vapour. Using GC/MS we identified 55 soil-derived compounds via the verification of fragmentation patterns and retention times against authentic standards.

Data analysis confirmed our hypothesis that soil fumigated with chloroform would have an increased metabolic response to bacterial lysing and therefore a greater diversity and quantity of rts extracted from the soil. To elucidate the fate of cell derived metabolites (ie - whether they bind to soil matrices etc), unlabelled extracts of *Pseudomonas stutzerii* RCH2 soil cultures were added to soil samples and analyzed using $^{13}$C labeled extract from *Pseudomonas stutzerii* RCH2 culture, with a wealth of highly labelled metabolites readily differentiated from background endogenous soil metabolites thereby enabling us to carry out a direct comparison between the freshly derived bacterial lysate and the rts remaining recoverable within the soil environment.

document.input:
Microbiological cycling of soil organic matter (SOM) is a fundamental yet poorly understood pillar of the global carbon cycle. Current research suggests that a significant portion of SOM—particularly the dissolved organic matter (DOM) fraction—consists of small molecules derived from lysed cells and microbial metabolites. Because the water-extractable organic matter (WEOM) fraction represents the most biologically accessible pool of SOM, it is a critical focus for study.

Using gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) profiling, we identified 55 metabolites by validating their fragmentation patterns and retention times against authentic standards.

To simulate the fate of cell-derived compounds, we spiked soil samples with a $^{13}$C-labeled extract from the soil bacterium *Pseudomonas stutzerii* RCH2. This produced highly enriched metabolites that were easily distinguished from endogenous soil compounds, allowing us to directly compare the composition of a fresh bacterial lysate with the fraction that remains recoverable from the soil matrix.
